testcases.title: Create order successfully with one available SKU
preconditions: Store A is active, verified, ordering-enabled, and not restricted by whitelist.
SKU S1 is available at unit price 100.
steps: Send POST /orders with Idempotency-Key K1 and body {store_id:"StoreA", items:[{sku_id:"S1", quantity:1}]}.
Capture order_id, snapshot_items, totals, and meta.request_id from the response.
Call GET /orders/{order_id} as the same user.
expected: POST /orders returns HTTP 201 with order status CREATED and a new order_id.
snapshot_items contains S1 with unit_price 100, quantity 1, and line_total 100; totals.subtotal and totals.total_amount are 100 and fees_total is 0.
GET /orders/{order_id} returns HTTP 200 with the same CREATED order data.

testcases.title: Create order successfully with multiple items and correct totals
preconditions: Store A is active, verified, and ordering-enabled.
SKU S1 price is 100 and SKU S2 price is 50; both are available.
steps: Send POST /orders with Idempotency-Key K2 and body {store_id:"StoreA", items:[{sku_id:"S1", quantity:2}, {sku_id:"S2", quantity:3}]}.
Capture order_id and the returned snapshot_items and totals.
Call GET /orders/{order_id}.
expected: POST /orders returns HTTP 201 with order status CREATED.
snapshot_items preserves S1 quantity 2 and S2 quantity 3, and totals.subtotal and totals.total_amount equal 350 with fees_total 0.
GET /orders/{order_id} returns HTTP 200 and matches the creation snapshot and totals.

testcases.title: Order snapshot keeps original price after catalog price changes
preconditions: Store A is active and eligible to accept orders.
SKU S1 is available at unit price 100 before the order is created.
steps: Create an order with POST /orders using Idempotency-Key K3 for {sku_id:"S1", quantity:1}.
Change the catalog price of SKU S1 to 120 in downstream menu data.
Call GET /orders/{order_id} for the existing order.
expected: The create call returns HTTP 201 and the order starts in CREATED.
GET /orders/{order_id} returns HTTP 200 and snapshot_items[0].unit_price remains 100.
Order totals remain based on the original snapshot price and do not change to 120.

testcases.title: Retry create order with the same idempotency key returns the same order
preconditions: Store A is active, verified, ordering-enabled, and SKU S1 is available.
steps: Send POST /orders with Idempotency-Key K4 and payload P4.
Retry POST /orders with the same Idempotency-Key K4 and the identical payload P4.
Call GET /orders/{order_id} using the order_id returned by both responses.
expected: Both create responses return the same order_id and do not create a second logical order.
Only one order row exists for idempotency key K4.
GET /orders/{order_id} returns one CREATED order with the expected snapshot.

testcases.title: Create order rejects idempotency replay mismatch
preconditions: Store A is active, verified, ordering-enabled, and SKUs S1 and S2 are available.
steps: Send POST /orders with Idempotency-Key K5 and payload {store_id:"StoreA", items:[{sku_id:"S1", quantity:1}]}.
Replay POST /orders with the same Idempotency-Key K5 but change the payload to {store_id:"StoreA", items:[{sku_id:"S2", quantity:1}]}.
Inspect the second response and query the order record created by the first request.
expected: The second request returns HTTP 409 with error code IDEMPOTENCY_REPLAY_MISMATCH.
The original order remains unchanged.
No second order is created for key K5.

testcases.title: Create order fails when Idempotency-Key header is missing
preconditions: Store A is active, verified, ordering-enabled, and SKU S1 is available.
steps: Send POST /orders without Idempotency-Key and with a valid order body.
Capture the error payload and HTTP status.
Search order storage for a row matching the submitted request.
expected: POST /orders returns HTTP 400 with a validation error and request_id.
No order is created for the request.
No partial snapshot or totals are persisted.

testcases.title: Create order is blocked when ordering is disabled
preconditions: Admin runtime control ORDERING_ENABLED is OFF.
Store A and the requesting user are otherwise eligible to order.
steps: Send POST /orders with Idempotency-Key K7 and a valid body for Store A.
Capture the error response.
Verify storage does not contain a new order for the request.
expected: POST /orders returns HTTP 403 with error code ORDERING_DISABLED.
No order_id is created.
The error payload includes request_id.

testcases.title: Create order is blocked when store is not verified
preconditions: Store B exists with verification status false.
Ordering is enabled globally.
steps: Send POST /orders for Store B with Idempotency-Key K8 and a valid item list.
Capture the error response.
Check persistence for any created order row.
expected: POST /orders returns HTTP 403 with error code STORE_NOT_VERIFIED.
No order is created.
The error payload includes request_id.

testcases.title: Create order is blocked when store is inactive
preconditions: Store C exists with inactive status.
Ordering is enabled globally.
steps: Send POST /orders for Store C with Idempotency-Key K9 and a valid body.
Capture the error response.
Verify no order record exists for the call.
expected: POST /orders returns HTTP 403 with error code STORE_INACTIVE.
No order is created.
The error payload includes request_id.

testcases.title: Create order fails when any requested SKU is unavailable
preconditions: Store A is eligible to accept orders.
SKU S1 is unavailable.
steps: Send POST /orders with Idempotency-Key K10 and body {store_id:"StoreA", items:[{sku_id:"S1", quantity:1}]}.
Capture the error payload and HTTP status.
Verify no order row was created.
expected: POST /orders returns HTTP 409 with error code ITEM_UNAVAILABLE.
No order is created.
The error response includes request_id.
testcases.notes: Unavailable items cannot be snapshotted into an order.

testcases.title: Create order rejects zero and non-integer quantities
preconditions: Store A is eligible to accept orders.
SKU S1 is available.
steps: Send POST /orders with Idempotency-Key K11A and quantity 0 for SKU S1.
Send POST /orders with Idempotency-Key K11B and quantity 1.5 for SKU S1.
Inspect both responses and verify storage.
expected: Both requests return HTTP 400 with error code INVALID_QUANTITY.
No order is created for either invalid request.
Each error response includes request_id.

testcases.title: Get order detail returns contract fields and success envelope metadata
preconditions: An order exists in status CREATED for user U12.
steps: Call GET /orders/{order_id} as user U12.
Inspect response fields in data and meta.
Compare the body against the read-model contract.
expected: GET /orders/{order_id} returns HTTP 200.
The response includes order_id, store_id, user_id, status, snapshot_items, totals, created_at, meta.request_id, and meta.server_time.
payment_status is null or absent until a payment attempt exists.

testcases.title: Get order detail is forbidden for a different user
preconditions: Order O13 belongs to user U13A.
User U13B is authenticated but is not the order owner.
steps: Call GET /orders/O13 as user U13B.
Capture the error response and HTTP status.
Inspect the body for leaked order content.
expected: GET /orders/O13 returns HTTP 403.
The response does not expose snapshot_items, totals, or other order data.
The error payload includes request_id.

testcases.title: Create payment attempt successfully from a CREATED order
preconditions: Order O14 exists in status CREATED.
PAYMENT_ENABLED is ON.
steps: Send POST /orders/O14/payments with Idempotency-Key P14.
Capture payment_attempt_id, payment_status, expires_at, and order status from the response.
Call GET /orders/O14.
expected: POST /orders/O14/payments returns HTTP 201 and creates a payment attempt with payment_status PENDING.
The order status changes from CREATED to PAYMENT_PENDING and expires_at is about 10 minutes after creation.
GET /orders/O14 returns HTTP 200 and shows latest payment_status PENDING with order status PAYMENT_PENDING.

testcases.title: Retry create payment with the same idempotency key returns the same attempt
preconditions: Order O15 exists in status CREATED.
PAYMENT_ENABLED is ON.
steps: Send POST /orders/O15/payments with Idempotency-Key P15.
Retry POST /orders/O15/payments with the same Idempotency-Key P15 and the same request body.
Inspect payment_attempt_id values and query payment attempts for O15.
expected: Both responses resolve to the same payment_attempt_id.
Only one active PENDING payment attempt exists for O15.
The order remains in PAYMENT_PENDING.

testcases.title: Create payment rejects idempotency replay mismatch
preconditions: Order O16 exists in status CREATED.
steps: Send POST /orders/O16/payments with Idempotency-Key P16 and payload A.
Replay POST /orders/O16/payments with the same Idempotency-Key P16 but modified payload B.
Inspect the second response and the stored payment attempt.
expected: The second request returns HTTP 409 with error code IDEMPOTENCY_REPLAY_MISMATCH.
The original payment attempt remains the only attempt tied to key P16.
The order state is unchanged from the first successful call.

testcases.title: Create payment fails when Idempotency-Key header is missing
preconditions: Order O17 exists in status CREATED.
steps: Send POST /orders/O17/payments without Idempotency-Key.
Capture the error response and HTTP status.
Verify that no payment attempt was created.
expected: POST /orders/O17/payments returns HTTP 400 with a validation error and request_id.
No payment attempt is created.
Order O17 remains in CREATED.
testcases.notes: Idempotency key is required for payment creation.

testcases.title: Create payment is blocked when payment is disabled
preconditions: Order O18 exists in status CREATED.
Admin runtime control PAYMENT_ENABLED is OFF.
steps: Send POST /orders/O18/payments with Idempotency-Key P18.
Capture the error response.
Verify no payment attempt exists for O18.
expected: POST /orders/O18/payments returns HTTP 403 with error code PAYMENT_DISABLED.
No payment attempt is created.
Order O18 remains in CREATED.

testcases.title: Create payment is blocked for cancelled and already-paid orders
preconditions: Order O19A exists in status CANCELLED.
Order O19B exists in status PAID with latest payment_status SUCCEEDED.
steps: Send POST /orders/O19A/payments with Idempotency-Key P19A.
Send POST /orders/O19B/payments with Idempotency-Key P19B.
Inspect both responses and query payment attempts for O19A and O19B.
expected: Both requests return HTTP 409 with ORDER_INVALID_STATE or PAYMENT_ALREADY_SUCCEEDED semantics.
No new payment attempt is created for either order.
Order O19A remains CANCELLED and order O19B remains PAID.
testcases.notes: Invalid state protection for payment creation.

testcases.title: Client timeout during payment creation is safe to retry
preconditions: Order O20 exists in status CREATED.
PAYMENT_ENABLED is ON.
steps: Send POST /orders/O20/payments with Idempotency-Key P20 and simulate a client-side timeout after the server receives the request.
Retry POST /orders/O20/payments with the same Idempotency-Key P20.
Inspect the returned payment_attempt_id and query payment attempts for O20.
expected: The retry returns the original payment attempt instead of creating a second one.
Only one active payment attempt exists for O20.
The recovered order state is PAYMENT_PENDING.
testcases.notes: Timeout recovery without duplicate money movement.

testcases.title: Order detail does not show paid state while payment is pending
preconditions: Order O21 is in status PAYMENT_PENDING.
The latest payment attempt for O21 is in payment_status PENDING.
steps: Call GET /orders/O21 during the pending window.
Inspect status, payment_status, paid_at, and any client-facing paid indicator fields.
Compare the returned values against the read-model rules.
expected: GET /orders/O21 returns HTTP 200 with order status PAYMENT_PENDING and payment_status PENDING.
paid_at is null or absent.
The order is not represented as PAID anywhere in the read model.

testcases.title: Payment timeout job does not expire an order before the 10-minute boundary
preconditions: Order O22 is in PAYMENT_PENDING.
Its latest payment attempt was created 9 minutes 59 seconds ago.
steps: Run the payment timeout job.
Call GET /orders/O22.
Inspect the latest payment attempt and notification records.
expected: The job does not expire the order before the boundary.
Order O22 remains PAYMENT_PENDING and the latest payment attempt remains PENDING.
No ORDER_EXPIRED notification is emitted.

testcases.title: Payment timeout job expires a pending payment after 10 minutes
preconditions: Order O23 is in PAYMENT_PENDING.
Its latest payment attempt is older than 10 minutes.
steps: Run the payment timeout job.
Call GET /orders/O23.
Inspect payment records and emitted notifications.
expected: The latest payment attempt becomes EXPIRED.
Order O23 moves to EXPIRED.
Exactly one ORDER_EXPIRED notification is emitted for O23.

testcases.title: Stripe success webhook moves PAYMENT_PENDING order to PAID and keeps read models consistent
preconditions: Order O24 is in PAYMENT_PENDING with one pending payment attempt.
A valid Stripe signature and success event can be produced for that attempt.
steps: Send POST /webhooks/stripe with success event E24 for the payment attempt and a valid signature.
Capture the webhook response.
Call GET /orders/O24 and inspect the latest payment attempt record.
expected: The webhook returns HTTP 200.
The latest payment_status becomes SUCCEEDED, the order status becomes PAID, and paid_at is populated.
Order totals remain unchanged from the original snapshot and payment and order read models show the same successful outcome.

testcases.title: Stripe failed webhook returns PAYMENT_PENDING order to CREATED and keeps read models consistent
preconditions: Order O25 is in PAYMENT_PENDING with one pending payment attempt.
A valid Stripe signature and failed event can be produced for that attempt.
steps: Send POST /webhooks/stripe with failed event E25 for the payment attempt and a valid signature.
Capture the webhook response.
Call GET /orders/O25 and inspect the latest payment attempt record.
expected: The webhook returns HTTP 200.
The latest payment_status becomes FAILED and the order status becomes CREATED.
paid_at remains null or absent and payment and order read models show the same failed outcome.

testcases.title: Second payment attempt is blocked after a payment already succeeded
preconditions: Order O26 is already in status PAID.
The latest payment attempt for O26 has payment_status SUCCEEDED.
steps: Send POST /orders/O26/payments with Idempotency-Key P26.
Capture the error response.
Query payment attempts for O26.
expected: POST /orders/O26/payments returns HTTP 409 with PAYMENT_ALREADY_SUCCEEDED or ORDER_INVALID_STATE semantics.
No new payment attempt is created.
Exactly one successful payment remains linked to O26.

testcases.title: Stripe webhook rejects invalid signature
preconditions: Order O27 is in PAYMENT_PENDING with a pending payment attempt.
steps: Send POST /webhooks/stripe with a malformed or invalid signature header.
Capture the HTTP status and body.
Call GET /orders/O27 afterward.
expected: The webhook returns HTTP 400.
Order O27 remains PAYMENT_PENDING.
The latest payment attempt remains PENDING and no side effects are emitted.

testcases.title: Webhook dedupe by event id is a no-op for success and failed events
preconditions: Order O28A is in PAYMENT_PENDING and can receive valid success event E28A.
Order O28B is in PAYMENT_PENDING and can receive valid failed event E28B.
steps: Send success event E28A twice to POST /webhooks/stripe for O28A.
Send failed event E28B twice to POST /webhooks/stripe for O28B.
Inspect order states, payment records, dedupe records, and emitted side effects.
expected: All webhook deliveries return HTTP 200.
For each order, only the first delivery changes state and the second delivery with the same event id is a no-op.
No duplicate notification, audit, refund, or payment side effect is created for either repeated event id.

testcases.title: Failed webhook arriving after a success does not regress a paid order
preconditions: Order O29 has already moved to PAID from a valid success webhook.
A later failed webhook for the same underlying payment attempt is available.
steps: Confirm O29 is PAID after the valid success webhook.
Send the later failed webhook for the same payment attempt to POST /webhooks/stripe.
Inspect the final order state, payment state, and audit timeline.
expected: The later webhook returns HTTP 200 but does not regress O29.
Order O29 remains PAID and no refund is created.
An audit record captures the ignored or reconciled out-of-order event.
testcases.notes: Protects against out-of-order webhook state drift.

testcases.title: Late success webhook for terminal orders triggers immediate full refund
preconditions: Order O30A is terminal in CANCELLED with no prior successful payment recorded.
Order O30B is terminal in REJECTED with no prior successful payment recorded.
Order O30C is terminal in EXPIRED with no prior successful payment recorded.
steps: Send valid late payment-success webhooks for O30A, O30B, and O30C to POST /webhooks/stripe.
Capture each webhook response.
Inspect order detail, payment records, refund records, audit entries, and alerting for all three orders.
expected: Each webhook returns HTTP 200 and records the payment outcome as SUCCEEDED for traceability.
Each terminal order immediately starts one full refund flow and keeps exactly one refund record.
All three orders remain terminal, do not re-enter fulfillment, and generate audit and alert artifacts for the late-webhook incident.

testcases.title: Duplicate late success webhook does not create a second refund or second alert
preconditions: Order O31 is terminal and has already processed one late success webhook.
The first late success webhook already started the refund flow for O31.
steps: Replay the same late success webhook event id for O31.
Inspect refund records, audit entries, and alert sink for O31.
Confirm the terminal order state after the replay.
expected: The replay returns HTTP 200 and is treated as a no-op.
Only one refund record remains associated with O31.
No duplicate alert or duplicate audit side effect is created for the repeated event id.

testcases.title: Burst duplicate success webhook deliveries keep a single financial outcome
preconditions: Order O32 is in PAYMENT_PENDING with one pending payment attempt.
steps: Fire 20 concurrent copies of the same valid success event id at POST /webhooks/stripe for O32.
Wait for processing to settle.
Inspect order status, payment records, refund records, notifications, and dedupe data.
expected: Order O32 ends in PAID exactly once.
There is one successful payment outcome and no refund is created.
Only one ORDER_PAID-style terminal payment notification is emitted.

testcases.title: Merchant advances a paid order through the full fulfillment lifecycle
preconditions: Order O33 exists in status PAID.
Merchant M33 belongs to the same store as O33.
steps: Send POST /merchant/orders/O33/accept.
Send POST /merchant/orders/O33/status with next_status PREPARING, then READY, then COMPLETED.
Fetch the final order detail and audit timeline.
expected: Each merchant action returns HTTP 200.
The order transitions in order from PAID to ACCEPTED to PREPARING to READY to COMPLETED.
The audit timeline records each forward state transition exactly once.

testcases.title: Merchant rejects a paid order with reason and refund is triggered
preconditions: Order O34 exists in status PAID.
Merchant M34 belongs to the same store as O34.
steps: Send POST /merchant/orders/O34/reject with body {reason_code:"OUT_OF_STOCK", note:"optional"}.
Capture the response and order status.
Inspect refund records and the audit timeline.
expected: POST /merchant/orders/O34/reject returns HTTP 200 and order status REJECTED.
A refund workflow starts and exactly one refund record exists for O34.
The audit timeline captures the reject reason and actor.

testcases.title: Merchant reject fails when reason_code is missing
preconditions: Order O35 exists in status PAID.
Merchant M35 belongs to the same store as O35.
steps: Send POST /merchant/orders/O35/reject without reason_code.
Capture the error response.
Inspect order detail and refund records after the call.
expected: POST /merchant/orders/O35/reject returns HTTP 400 with error code REJECT_REASON_REQUIRED.
Order O35 remains PAID.
No refund record is created.

testcases.title: Merchant cannot accept or reject an order that is not paid
preconditions: Order O36 exists in status CREATED.
Merchant M36 belongs to the same store as O36.
steps: Send POST /merchant/orders/O36/accept.
Send POST /merchant/orders/O36/reject with a valid reason_code.
Inspect order detail, refund records, and audit entries.
expected: Both merchant calls return HTTP 409 with ORDER_NOT_PAID or ORDER_INVALID_STATE semantics.
Order O36 remains CREATED after both requests.
No refund record and no merchant terminal action are created.

testcases.title: Invalid fulfillment status transitions are rejected
preconditions: Order O37A is in ACCEPTED.
Order O37B is in READY.
Order O37C is in PAID.
Order O37D is in COMPLETED.
steps: Send POST /merchant/orders/O37A/status with next_status READY and then COMPLETED.
Send POST /merchant/orders/O37B/status with next_status PREPARING, and send POST /merchant/orders/O37C/status with next_status PREPARING.
Send POST /merchant/orders/O37D/status with any next_status and inspect all affected orders.
expected: Each invalid transition returns HTTP 409 with STATUS_SEQUENCE_INVALID or ORDER_INVALID_STATE semantics.
All orders remain in their original statuses.
No invalid transition is recorded in the audit timeline.
testcases.notes: Required invalid-state coverage for fulfillment flow.

testcases.title: Merchant from another store is forbidden from updating order status
preconditions: Order O38 belongs to Store A and is in ACCEPTED.
Merchant M38 is authenticated but belongs to Store B.
steps: Send POST /merchant/orders/O38/status with next_status PREPARING as merchant M38.
Capture the error response.
Inspect order detail and audit records.
expected: The request returns HTTP 403 with error code STORE_SCOPE_FORBIDDEN.
Order O38 remains ACCEPTED.
No audit transition is created for the forbidden action.

testcases.title: User cancels a paid but not yet accepted order successfully
preconditions: Order O39 exists in status PAID.
No merchant acceptance has happened for O39.
steps: Send POST /orders/O39/cancel with Idempotency-Key C39.
Capture the response and order status.
Inspect refund records and the audit timeline.
expected: POST /orders/O39/cancel returns HTTP 200 and the order status becomes CANCELLED.
A refund workflow starts and exactly one refund record exists for O39.
The audit timeline records the user cancellation.

testcases.title: User cannot cancel orders in CREATED or ACCEPTED
preconditions: Order O40A exists in status CREATED.
Order O40B exists in status ACCEPTED.
steps: Send POST /orders/O40A/cancel.
Send POST /orders/O40B/cancel.
Inspect both responses, final order states, and refund records.
expected: Both requests return HTTP 409 with error code ORDER_INVALID_STATE.
Order O40A remains CREATED and order O40B remains ACCEPTED.
No refund record is created for either order.
testcases.notes: Invalid state coverage for user cancellation.

testcases.title: Retry user cancel does not create a duplicate refund
preconditions: Order O41 exists in status PAID and has not been accepted.
steps: Send POST /orders/O41/cancel once and confirm cancellation has started.
Retry POST /orders/O41/cancel for the same order.
Inspect order detail, refund records, and emitted side effects.
expected: Order O41 remains CANCELLED after the retry.
Only one refund record exists for O41.
The retry does not create a second refund or duplicate cancellation side effect.

testcases.title: Retry merchant reject does not create a second refund record
preconditions: Order O42 was already rejected once and has one refund record.
steps: Replay POST /merchant/orders/O42/reject with a valid reason_code.
Inspect the response, order detail, and refund records.
Check notification and audit counts.
expected: Order O42 stays REJECTED.
Refund count remains one.
The retry does not start a second refund workflow or create duplicate terminal side effects.

testcases.title: Cancel request after refund already succeeded is rejected
preconditions: Order O43 is already CANCELLED with refund_status SUCCEEDED.
steps: Send POST /orders/O43/cancel again.
Capture the error response.
Inspect refund records and the final order state.
expected: The request returns HTTP 409 with REFUND_ALREADY_DONE or equivalent terminal-refund semantics.
Refund count remains one.
Order O43 remains CANCELLED.

testcases.title: SLA job auto-cancels a paid order after more than 15 minutes without acceptance
preconditions: Order O44 is in status PAID.
paid_at is older than 15 minutes and the order has not been accepted.
steps: Run the merchant SLA auto-action job.
Call GET /orders/O44 or inspect the admin detail read model.
Inspect refund records, audit entries, and notifications.
expected: Order O44 becomes CANCELLED automatically.
A full refund flow is triggered exactly once.
Audit shows a system actor and SLA timeout reason for the auto-cancel.

testcases.title: SLA job does not auto-cancel before the 15-minute boundary
preconditions: Order O45 is in status PAID.
paid_at is 14 minutes 59 seconds ago and the order is still unaccepted.
steps: Run the merchant SLA auto-action job.
Fetch order detail for O45.
Inspect refund and notification records.
expected: Order O45 remains PAID.
No refund record is created.
No ORDER_CANCELLED event is emitted.

testcases.title: Scheduler jobs skip orders already ACCEPTED or CANCELLED
preconditions: Order O46A was paid earlier and is now ACCEPTED before the SLA job runs.
Order O46B is already CANCELLED before timeout or SLA sweeps run.
steps: Run the merchant SLA auto-action job and the payment timeout job.
Fetch final order detail for O46A and O46B.
Inspect refund records and audit timelines for both orders.
expected: Order O46A remains ACCEPTED and order O46B remains CANCELLED.
No new refund is started for either order.
Scheduler jobs do not add conflicting terminal actions.

testcases.title: Refund failure is reflected on the order and audited
preconditions: Order O47 can enter a refund-required path such as merchant reject or user cancel.
The downstream refund provider is configured to fail.
steps: Trigger the refund-required action for O47.
Wait for refund processing to finish with failure.
Inspect order detail, refund record, and audit timeline.
expected: refund_status becomes FAILED.
Exactly one refund record exists with failed outcome details.
The audit timeline records the refund failure.

testcases.title: Refund notifications emit requested and exactly one terminal outcome without duplication
preconditions: Notification sink and dedupe tracking are observable.
Refund scenarios can be triggered in both success and failure modes.
steps: Trigger one refund-required path that completes successfully.
Trigger a separate refund-required path that ends in refund failure.
Inspect emitted notifications and dedupe keys for both orders.
expected: REFUND_REQUESTED is emitted when each refund starts.
Exactly one terminal refund notification is emitted per order: REFUND_SUCCEEDED or REFUND_FAILED.
Duplicate notifications are suppressible by a stable dedupe_key.

testcases.title: Admin order list applies combined filters correctly
preconditions: Admin user A49 is authenticated.
Seed data includes orders across multiple stores, statuses, payment states, refund states, and keywords.
steps: Call GET /admin/orders with date range, store_id, status, payment_status, refund_status, and keyword q.
Inspect the returned rows and pagination metadata.
Cross-check included and excluded orders against the filter criteria.
expected: GET /admin/orders returns HTTP 200.
Only orders matching every supplied filter are returned.
Returned rows include at least order_id or code, store_id, status, total_amount, created_at, paid_at, and refund_status.

testcases.title: Admin order detail shows snapshot items, payment attempts, refund record, and audit timeline
preconditions: Admin user A50 is authenticated.
Order O50 has snapshot_items, at least one payment attempt, and a refund record.
steps: Call GET /admin/orders/O50.
Inspect the response body sections.
Compare the response with the underlying order, payment, refund, and audit records.
expected: GET /admin/orders/O50 returns HTTP 200.
Admin detail includes snapshot items, totals, payment attempts, refund record, and complete audit timeline.
The returned data matches the underlying records.

testcases.title: Non-admin user cannot access admin order list or admin order detail
preconditions: User U51 is authenticated without admin privileges.
Order O51 exists.
steps: Call GET /admin/orders as U51.
Call GET /admin/orders/O51 as U51.
Inspect both responses for leaked data.
expected: Both requests return HTTP 403.
No admin list data, snapshot data, refund data, or audit data is exposed.
Each error payload includes request_id.

testcases.title: Admin runtime control changes are audited and take effect immediately
preconditions: Admin user A52 is authenticated and can update runtime controls.
Store A is otherwise eligible to accept orders.
User U52A is intended to be whitelisted and user U52B is intended to be non-whitelisted.
steps: Send POST /admin/flags to set ORDERING_ENABLED to OFF, then attempt POST /orders as U52A for Store A with Idempotency-Key K52A.
Send POST /admin/flags to set ORDERING_ENABLED to ON and PAYMENT_ENABLED to OFF, then send POST /admin/whitelist to enable whitelist mode for Store A and U52A only.
Create an order as U52A, attempt POST /orders/{order_id}/payments with Idempotency-Key P52A, attempt POST /orders as U52B with Idempotency-Key K52B, and inspect the audit log for all control changes.
expected: All admin control updates return HTTP 200 and are written to audit with actor, timestamp, old value, and new value.
The create-order attempt while ORDERING_ENABLED is OFF returns HTTP 403 with ORDERING_DISABLED.
After the updates, whitelisted U52A can create an order, payment creation returns HTTP 403 with PAYMENT_DISABLED, and non-whitelisted U52B is rejected with HTTP 403 and WHITELIST_REQUIRED.
testcases.notes: Combines auditability and immediate-effect checks for runtime controls.

testcases.title: Merchant new-order notification is emitted only when the order becomes PAID
preconditions: Notification sink is observable for merchant push events.
Order O53 can be created and moved through payment end to end.
steps: Create order O53 and inspect the notification sink while the order is in CREATED.
Create a payment attempt for O53 and inspect the notification sink while the order is in PAYMENT_PENDING.
Send a valid success webhook for O53 and inspect the notification sink again.
expected: No merchant new-order notification is emitted while O53 is in CREATED or PAYMENT_PENDING.
Exactly one merchant new-order notification is emitted when O53 becomes PAID.
The notification references the correct order_id and a stable dedupe key.

testcases.title: Duplicate success webhook does not emit duplicate ORDER_PAID notification
preconditions: Order O54 is in PAYMENT_PENDING.
Notification sink records ORDER_PAID events with dedupe_key.
steps: Send one valid payment-success webhook for O54 and confirm the order becomes PAID.
Replay the same webhook event id for O54.
Inspect ORDER_PAID notifications in the sink.
expected: Only one ORDER_PAID notification exists for O54.
The replayed webhook does not create a second notification.
The notification dedupe_key remains stable for client-side deduplication.

testcases.title: Concurrent create-order retries collapse to one order
preconditions: Store A is eligible to accept orders and SKU S1 is available.
The client can issue concurrent requests with the same idempotency key.
steps: Fire 10 concurrent POST /orders requests with the same Idempotency-Key K55 and identical payload.
Wait for all responses.
Query orders by idempotency key K55 and inspect returned order_ids.
expected: All successful responses reference the same order_id.
Only one order row is created for K55.
Snapshot items and totals remain correct.

testcases.title: Concurrent create-payment retries collapse to one payment attempt
preconditions: Order O56 exists in status CREATED.
The client can issue concurrent payment-init requests with the same idempotency key.
steps: Fire 10 concurrent POST /orders/O56/payments requests with the same Idempotency-Key P56.
Wait for all responses.
Inspect payment attempts and order detail for O56.
expected: All successful responses reference the same payment_attempt_id.
Only one payment attempt is created for O56.
Order O56 ends in PAYMENT_PENDING with one active latest payment.
